title: University of Göttingen

infobox.value: [German Excellence Universities](/wiki/German_Excellence_Universities "German Excellence Universities"), [U15](/wiki/U15_(German_Universities) "U15 (German Universities)"), [EUA](/wiki/European_University_Association "European University Association"), U4 Network, [Guild of European Research-Intensive Universities](/wiki/Guild_of_European_Research-Intensive_Universities "Guild of European Research-Intensive Universities")

([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Georg-August-Universität Göttingen*, commonly referred to as **Georgia Augusta**), is a [public research university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in the city of [Göttingen](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen "Göttingen"), Lower Saxony, Germany. Founded in 1734 by [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"), King of Great Britain and [Elector of Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover"), it began instruction in 1737 and is recognized as the oldest university in Lower Saxony. Recognized for its historic and traditional significance, the university has affiliations with 47 Nobel Prize winners by its own count.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The **University of Göttingen**, officially the **Georg August University of Göttingen** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Georg-August-Universität Göttingen*, commonly referred to as **Georgia Augusta**), is a [public research university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in the city of [Göttingen](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen "Göttingen"), Lower Saxony, Germany. Founded in 1734 by [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"), King of Great Britain and [Elector of Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover"), it began instruction in 1737 and is recognized as the oldest university in Lower Saxony. Recognized for its historic and traditional significance, the university has affiliations with 47 Nobel Prize winners by its own count.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The University of Göttingen reached its academic peak from the late 19th to early 20th century, establishing itself as a major international center for [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") and [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"). During this period, scholars such as [David Hilbert](/wiki/David_Hilbert "David Hilbert"), [Felix Klein](/wiki/Felix_Klein "Felix Klein"), [Max Born](/wiki/Max_Born "Max Born"), and [Ludwig Prandtl](/wiki/Ludwig_Prandtl "Ludwig Prandtl") conducted influential research in mathematics, [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"), and [aerodynamics](/wiki/Aerodynamics "Aerodynamics"). The university attracted international students, including prominent Americans such as [Edward Everett](/wiki/Edward_Everett "Edward Everett"), [George Bancroft](/wiki/George_Bancroft "George Bancroft"), [John Lothrop Motley](/wiki/John_Lothrop_Motley "John Lothrop Motley"), and [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer"). This prominence was severely disrupted by the [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") rise to power in 1933, when the "[great purge](/wiki/Law_for_the_Restoration_of_the_Professional_Civil_Service "Law for the Restoration of the Professional Civil Service")" resulted in the dismissal or emigration of numerous faculty members, including many of Jewish origin or those opposed to the regime. The university was subsequently reopened under [British control](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany") in 1945 and began a process of academic reconstruction.

Today, the University of Göttingen is a member of the [U15 Group](/wiki/U15_(German_Universities) "U15 (German Universities)") of major German research universities. It is also a part of prominent international and European academic networks such as [The Guild](/wiki/Guild_of_European_Research-Intensive_Universities "Guild of European Research-Intensive Universities"), the ENLIGHT alliance, and the Hekksagon network. The university maintains close collaborations with leading Göttingen-based research institutions such as [Max Planck Society](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society"), the [Leibniz Association](/wiki/Leibniz_Association "Leibniz Association"), the [Fraunhofer Society](/wiki/Fraunhofer_Society "Fraunhofer Society"), and the [Helmholtz Association](/wiki/Helmholtz_Association "Helmholtz Association"). With its extensive collection, the [Göttingen State and University Library](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_State_and_University_Library "Göttingen State and University Library") stands among Germany's largest libraries.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/George_II_of_Great_Britain_-_1730-50.jpg/250px-George_II_of_Great_Britain_-_1730-50.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_II_of_Great_Britain_-_1730-50.jpg)

King George II, founder and president of the university

### Inauguration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Inauguration")]

In 1734, King [George II of Great Britain](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") and [Elector of Hanover](/wiki/Elector_of_Hanover "Elector of Hanover"), commanded his Prime Minister in Hanover, Gerlach Adolph von Münchhausen, to establish a university in Göttingen to spread the ideals of the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"). [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") famously remarked, "Göttingen belongs neither to a State, nor to Germany, and is the University of Europe".[[8]](#cite_note-Kaleidoscope-8)

The initial university infrastructure was modest, comprising only a [riding hall](/wiki/Riding_hall "Riding hall") and a [fencing](/wiki/Fencing "Fencing") house, with lectures held in the [Paulinerkirche](/wiki/Paulinerkirche,_G%C3%B6ttingen "Paulinerkirche, Göttingen"), [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_order "Dominican order") monastery, or professors' homes. A university auditorium wasn't constructed until the 19th century.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Goettingen_-_Innenansicht_Paulinerkirche_%281748%29.png/250px-Goettingen_-_Innenansicht_Paulinerkirche_%281748%29.png)](/wiki/File:Goettingen_-_Innenansicht_Paulinerkirche_(1748).png)

King George II in the Pauliner Church in 1748

### 18th–19th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 18th–19th centuries")]

Throughout the 18th century, the University of Göttingen was renowned among German universities for its commitment to the free spirit and scientific exploration. [Georg Christoph Lichtenberg](/wiki/Georg_Christoph_Lichtenberg "Georg Christoph Lichtenberg"), a prominent scholar, held one of the first professorships dedicated to experimental physics in Germany from 1769 to 1799.[[10]](#cite_note-10) By 1812, Göttingen had established itself as a modern, internationally recognized university, boasting a library with over 200,000 volumes.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the first years of the University of Göttingen, it became known especially for its [Faculty of Law](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_University_Faculty_of_Law "Göttingen University Faculty of Law"). In the 18th century [Johann Stephan Pütter](/wiki/Johann_Stephan_P%C3%BCtter "Johann Stephan Pütter"), a scholar of public law at that time, taught [jus publicum](/wiki/Jus_publicum "Jus publicum") for half a century. The subject had attracted students such as [Klemens Wenzel Lothar von Metternich](/wiki/Klemens_Wenzel_Lothar_von_Metternich "Klemens Wenzel Lothar von Metternich"), later diplomat and Prime Minister of Austria, and [Wilhelm von Humboldt](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Humboldt "Wilhelm von Humboldt"), who later established the [University of Berlin](/wiki/University_of_Berlin "University of Berlin"). In the 19th century, [Gustav Hugo](/wiki/Gustav_Hugo "Gustav Hugo"), [Karl Friedrich Eichhorn](/wiki/Karl_Friedrich_Eichhorn "Karl Friedrich Eichhorn"), and [Georg Friedrich Sartorius](/wiki/Georg_Friedrich_Sartorius "Georg Friedrich Sartorius"), who taught law here, became the pioneers of the [German Historical School](/wiki/German_historical_school "German historical school") of Jurisprudence. At the time, Göttingen was a very popular place for the study of law in Germany: Even the great German poet [Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine") obtained a doctorate in law here in 1825. [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck"), the main creator and the first Chancellor of the second [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), also studied law in Göttingen in 1833: he lived in a tiny house on the "Wall", now known as "Bismarck Cottage". According to oral tradition, he lived there because his rowdiness had caused him to be banned from living within the city walls.[[12]](#cite_note-12) By the university's centenary in 1837, it was known as the "university of law", as the students enrolled by the faculty of law often made up more than half of the university's students.[[8]](#cite_note-Kaleidoscope-8) At the end of the 19th century, the famous civil law scholar [Rudolf von Jhering](/wiki/Rudolf_von_Jhering "Rudolf von Jhering"), who created the theory of "culpa in contrahendo" (fault in conclusion of a contract), remained a law professor in Göttingen until he died. [Lassa Francis Lawrence Oppenheim](/wiki/L._F._L._Oppenheim "L. F. L. Oppenheim"), known as the father of the modern discipline of international law and author of the famous two-volume "International Law: A Treatise", earned his doctorate in law from the University of Göttingen in 1881.

Likewise, the [Faculty of Theology](/wiki/Faculty_of_Theology,_University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen "Faculty of Theology, University of Göttingen") in conjunction with other orientalists and ancient historians across the university became an international center for the study of religion and antiquity.

In 1809, [Arthur Schopenhauer](/wiki/Arthur_Schopenhauer "Arthur Schopenhauer"), the German philosopher best known for his work *The World as Will and Representation*, became a student at the university, where he studied [metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics "Metaphysics") and [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") under [Gottlob Ernst Schulze](/wiki/Gottlob_Ernst_Schulze "Gottlob Ernst Schulze"), who advised him to concentrate on [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") and [Kant](/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

During this time, the University of Göttingen achieved renown for its critical work on history as well. An Enlightenment institution, it produced the [Göttingen school of history](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_school_of_history "Göttingen school of history"). Later, [Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber"), one of the most important theorists of the development of modern Western society, also studied history in Göttingen.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Goettingen_-_Kirchenzug_der_Studenten_anlaesslich_des_Universitaetsjubilaeums_%281837%29.png/250px-Goettingen_-_Kirchenzug_der_Studenten_anlaesslich_des_Universitaetsjubilaeums_%281837%29.png)](/wiki/File:Goettingen_-_Kirchenzug_der_Studenten_anlaesslich_des_Universitaetsjubilaeums_(1837).png)

Student parade – university anniversary 1837

The [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm"), the best-known storytellers of folktales like "[Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella "Cinderella")", "[The Frog Prince](/wiki/The_Frog_Prince "The Frog Prince")", "[Little Red Riding Hood](/wiki/Little_Red_Riding_Hood "Little Red Riding Hood")", "[Sleeping Beauty](/wiki/Sleeping_Beauty "Sleeping Beauty")", and "[Snow White](/wiki/Snow_White "Snow White")", taught here and compiled the first German dictionary.

However, political disturbances, in which both professors and students were implicated, lowered the attendance to 860 in 1834. The expulsion in 1837 of the seven professors – the so-called *Die [Göttinger Sieben](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttinger_Sieben "Göttinger Sieben")* (the Germanist [Wilhelm Eduard Albrecht](/wiki/Wilhelm_Eduard_Albrecht "Wilhelm Eduard Albrecht") (1800–1876), the historian [Friedrich Christoph Dahlmann](/wiki/Friedrich_Christoph_Dahlmann "Friedrich Christoph Dahlmann") (1785–1860), the orientalist [Georg Heinrich August Ewald](/wiki/Georg_Heinrich_August_Ewald "Georg Heinrich August Ewald") (1803–1875), the historian [Georg Gottfried Gervinus](/wiki/Georg_Gottfried_Gervinus "Georg Gottfried Gervinus") (1805–1875), the physicist [Wilhelm Eduard Weber](/wiki/Wilhelm_Eduard_Weber "Wilhelm Eduard Weber") (1804–1891), and the philologist brothers [Jakob](/wiki/Jakob_Grimm "Jakob Grimm") (1785–1863) and [Wilhelm Grimm](/wiki/Wilhelm_Grimm "Wilhelm Grimm") (1786–1859)) – for protesting against the revocation by [Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover](/wiki/Ernest_Augustus,_King_of_Hanover "Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover"), of the liberal constitution of 1833 hurt the reputation of the city and the university.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Turn of the 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Turn of the 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/G%C3%B6ttingen_asv2022-06_img42_Uni_Aula_Wilhelmsplatz.jpg/250px-G%C3%B6ttingen_asv2022-06_img42_Uni_Aula_Wilhelmsplatz.jpg)](/wiki/File:G%C3%B6ttingen_asv2022-06_img42_Uni_Aula_Wilhelmsplatz.jpg)

Alte Aula (*Great Hall*), also [Karzer](/wiki/Karzer "Karzer"), at Wilhelmsplatz (built in 1835–1837)

At the end of the 19th and beginning of the 20th century, the University of Göttingen achieved its academic peak. Göttingen maintained a strong focus on natural science, especially mathematics. The tradition began with [Carl Friedrich Gauss](/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Gauss "Carl Friedrich Gauss"), who was known as "the Prince of Mathematicians" and taught here in the 18th century. Thereafter, [Dirichlet](/wiki/Peter_Gustav_Lejeune_Dirichlet "Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet") and [Riemann](/wiki/Bernhard_Riemann "Bernhard Riemann") took over the chair successively and made significant contributions in the fields of algebra, geometry, and number theory. By 1900, [David Hilbert](/wiki/David_Hilbert "David Hilbert") and [Felix Klein](/wiki/Felix_Klein "Felix Klein") had attracted mathematicians from around the world to Göttingen, which made it a leading center of mathematics by the turn of the 20th century.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1903, its teaching staff numbered 121 and its students 1529. [Edmund Husserl](/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl"), a famous philosopher known as the father of [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy) "Phenomenology (philosophy)"), moved to Göttingen to teach. [Ludwig Prandtl](/wiki/Ludwig_Prandtl "Ludwig Prandtl") joined the University of Göttingen in 1904, and developed it into a leader in [fluid mechanics](/wiki/Fluid_mechanics "Fluid mechanics") and in [aerodynamics](/wiki/Aerodynamics "Aerodynamics") over the next two decades. In 1925, Prandtl was appointed as the director of the [Kaiser Wilhelm Institute](/wiki/Kaiser_Wilhelm_Institute "Kaiser Wilhelm Institute") for Fluid Mechanics. He introduced the concept of [boundary layer](/wiki/Boundary_layer "Boundary layer") and founded mathematical aerodynamics by calculating air flow in the down wind direction. Many of Prandtl's students went on to make fundamental contributions to aerodynamics. Between 1921 and 1933, the physics theory group was led by [Max Born](/wiki/Max_Born "Max Born"), who, during this time, became one of the three discoverers of the non-relativistic theory of [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"). He may also have been the first to propose its probabilistic relationship with classical physics. It was one of the main centers of the development of [modern physics](/wiki/Modern_physics "Modern physics"). [Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer"), the American scientist and "father of the atomic bomb", was one of [Max Born](/wiki/Max_Born "Max Born")'s most famous students and received his doctorate here.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/G%C3%B6ttingen_Aula_Konzert_H%C3%A4ndelfestspiele_2005.jpg/250px-G%C3%B6ttingen_Aula_Konzert_H%C3%A4ndelfestspiele_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:G%C3%B6ttingen_Aula_Konzert_H%C3%A4ndelfestspiele_2005.jpg)

The interior of the university Aula

During this time, the German language became an international academic language. A number of dissertations in the UK and the US had German titles. One might be considered having had a complete academic training only when one had studied in Germany. Thus, many American students were proud of having studied in Germany, and the University of Göttingen had profound impacts on the US. A number of American politicians, lawyers, historians and writers received their education from both [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard "Harvard") and Göttingen. For example, [Edward Everett](/wiki/Edward_Everett "Edward Everett"), once [Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") and President of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), stayed in Göttingen for two years of study. [George Ticknor](/wiki/George_Ticknor "George Ticknor") spent two years studying classics in Göttingen. Even [John Lothrop Motley](/wiki/John_Lothrop_Motley "John Lothrop Motley"), a diplomat and historian, had personal friendship with Otto von Bismarck during his two-year-long study in Göttingen. [George Bancroft](/wiki/George_Bancroft "George Bancroft"), a politician and historian, received his PhD from the University of Göttingen in 1820.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Mathematiker_1920_G%C3%B6ttingen.jpg/250px-Mathematiker_1920_G%C3%B6ttingen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mathematiker_1920_G%C3%B6ttingen.jpg)

Mathematicians celebrating Siegel's Ph.D. graduation, June 1920 at Göttingen: Grandjot, Bessel-Hagen, Rogosinski, Ness, Windau, Siegel (in the trolley), Walfisz, Krull, Emersleben, Kopfermann, Hedwig Wolff, Boskowits, Kneser.

### "Great purge" of 1933

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: \"Great purge\" of 1933")]

In the 1930s, the university became a focal point for the [Nazi](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") crackdown on "Jewish physics", as represented by the work of [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein"), in favor of "[German physics](/wiki/German_physics "German physics")". In what was later called the ["great purge" of 1933](/wiki/Law_for_the_Restoration_of_the_Professional_Civil_Service "Law for the Restoration of the Professional Civil Service"), academics including [Max Born](/wiki/Max_Born "Max Born"), [Victor Goldschmidt](/wiki/Victor_Goldschmidt "Victor Goldschmidt"), [James Franck](/wiki/James_Franck "James Franck"), [Eugene Wigner](/wiki/Eugene_Wigner "Eugene Wigner"), [Leó Szilárd](/wiki/Le%C3%B3_Szil%C3%A1rd "Leó Szilárd"), [Edward Teller](/wiki/Edward_Teller "Edward Teller"), [Edmund Landau](/wiki/Edmund_Landau "Edmund Landau"), [Emmy Noether](/wiki/Emmy_Noether "Emmy Noether"), and [Richard Courant](/wiki/Richard_Courant "Richard Courant") were expelled or fled. Most of them fled [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") for places like the United States, Canada, and the United Kingdom. Following the great purge, in 1934 [David Hilbert](/wiki/David_Hilbert "David Hilbert"), by then a symbol of German mathematics, was dining with [Bernhard Rust](/wiki/Bernhard_Rust "Bernhard Rust"), the Nazi minister of education. Rust asked, "How is mathematics at Göttingen, now that it is free from the Jewish influence?" Hilbert replied, "There is no mathematics in Göttingen anymore."[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Renovation after WWII

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Renovation after WWII")]

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the University of Göttingen was the first university in the [western Zones](/wiki/Allied_Occupation_Zones_in_Germany "Allied Occupation Zones in Germany") to be re-opened under British control in 1945. Göttingen's history of affiliation with numerous celebrities continued: [Jürgen Habermas](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Habermas "Jürgen Habermas"), a famous German philosopher and sociologist, pursued his study in Göttingen. Later, [Richard von Weizsäcker](/wiki/Richard_von_Weizs%C3%A4cker "Richard von Weizsäcker"), a late [President of Germany](/wiki/President_of_Germany "President of Germany"), earned his doctorate in law here. [Gerhard Schröder](/wiki/Gerhard_Schr%C3%B6der "Gerhard Schröder"), a former [Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany_(Federal_Republic) "Chancellor of Germany (Federal Republic)"), also graduated from the [Faculty of Law in Göttingen](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_University_Faculty_of_Law "Göttingen University Faculty of Law"). [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen"), [President of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission"), studied economics in Göttingen, where she met her husband [Heiko von der Leyen](/wiki/Heiko_von_der_Leyen "Heiko von der Leyen").

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Campus")]

The university is spread out in several locations around the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Grape_-_G%C3%B6ttinger_Universit%C3%A4ts-_und_Bibliotheksgeb%C3%A4ude_1815.jpg/250px-Grape_-_G%C3%B6ttinger_Universit%C3%A4ts-_und_Bibliotheksgeb%C3%A4ude_1815.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grape_-_G%C3%B6ttinger_Universit%C3%A4ts-_und_Bibliotheksgeb%C3%A4ude_1815.jpg)

The old Göttingen university campus and library building, called the Collegiate Building, c. 1815

The central university complex with the Central Library and *Mensa* (student refectory/dining hall) is located right next to the inner city and comprises the faculties for Theology, Social sciences, Law, Economics/Business Administration and Linguistics. The departments of Ancient History, Classics, various languages, Psychology and Philosophy are nearby. Located to the south of the city is the Faculty of Mathematics and Computer Science with its main building, the Mathematisches Institut, on the same street as the German Aerospace Center and the Max Planck Institute for Dynamics and Self-Organisation. In other parts of the city are the departments of Anthropology and Educational Sciences as well as the Medical Faculty with its associated hospitals.

Just north of the city a new scientific center has been built in which most of the natural sciences (chemistry, biology, plant pathology, agronomy, forestry, geology, physics, computer science) are now located, including the GZMB. Other institutes are set around the inner city.

The Pauliner Church, once the seat of the University Library in which [Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine"), the [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm"), and [Goethe](/wiki/Goethe "Goethe") worked

Closely linked with the university is the [Göttingen State and University Library](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_State_and_University_Library "Göttingen State and University Library") (German: Niedersächsische Staats- und Universitätsbibliothek Göttingen, or SUB Göttingen). With around 9 million media units and precious manuscripts, the library is designed for Göttingen University as well as the central library for the German State of Lower Saxony (with its central catalogue) and for the Göttingen Academy of Sciences, founded as the 'Royal Society for Sciences'.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Gardens")]

The university maintains three botanical gardens: the Alter Botanischer Garten der Universität Göttingen, the Neuer Botanischer Garten der Universität Göttingen, and the Forstbotanischer Garten und Pflanzengeographisches Arboretum der Universität Göttingen.

## Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Organisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Auditorium_G%C3%B6ttingen.jpg/250px-Auditorium_G%C3%B6ttingen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auditorium_G%C3%B6ttingen.jpg)

The old Auditorium Maximum (built in 1826–1865)

As of 2023, the university consists of 13 faculties and around 22,484 students are enrolled.[[19]](#cite_note-19) 535 professors and over 4,000 academic staff work at the university,[[20]](#cite_note-20) assisted by a technical and administrative staff of over 7,000. The post-war expansion of the university led to the establishment of a new, modern "university quarter" in the north of the city. The architecture of the old university can still be seen in the Auditorium Maximum (1826/1865) and the Great Hall (1835/1837) at Wilhelmsplatz.

Within the framework of the 2006–07 [German Universities Excellence Initiative](/wiki/German_Universities_Excellence_Initiative "German Universities Excellence Initiative"), it won funding for its future concept "Tradition, Innovation, Autonomy", its graduate school "Neurosciences and Molecular Biosciences", and its research cluster "Microscopy at the Nanometer Range". ### Historical reputation and impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Historical reputation and impact")]

The University of Göttingen is a traditional institution with a significant historical standing. This is substantiated by a scientific study on the historical development of world university rankings starting in 2013,[[29]](#cite_note-29) which examined a web dataset from 24 Wikipedia language versions, covering 59% of the world's population and accounting for 68% of all Wikipedia articles across 287 languages.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) This comprehensive analysis identified the most influential universities globally over the past ten centuries, resulting in the "2017 Wikipedia World University Rankings",[[33]](#cite_note-33) where the University of Göttingen ranked the 20th in terms of "web page rank" (search engine data),[[34]](#cite_note-34) the 8th on account of "chei rank" (interactive citation data),[[35]](#cite_note-35) and the 6th in the world on "2D rank" combining both categories of data.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

As of 2002, the University of Göttingen was associated with 44 Nobel laureates according to an official count released by the University of Göttingen in that year.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) By this number alone, the University of Göttingen ranked among the global top 15 universities. Recent Nobel laureates associated with the university are [Klaus Hasselmann](/wiki/Klaus_Hasselmann "Klaus Hasselmann") ([Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"), 2021), [Stefan Hell](/wiki/Stefan_Hell "Stefan Hell") ([Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), 2014), [Thomas C. Südhof](/wiki/Thomas_C._S%C3%BCdhof "Thomas C. Südhof") ([Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine"), 2013), and [Thomas Arthur Steitz](/wiki/Thomas_A._Steitz "Thomas A. Steitz") ([Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), 2009). Klaus Hasselmann received his PhD in physics from the University of Göttingen in 1957. Stefan Hell has been a lecturer (in Privatdozent capacity) at the University of Göttingen since 2004 and the director of the [Max Planck Institute for Biophysical Chemistry](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Biophysical_Chemistry "Max Planck Institute for Biophysical Chemistry") in the Göttingen Campus since 2002. [Thomas Südhof](/wiki/Thomas_C._S%C3%BCdhof "Thomas C. Südhof"), currently a professor at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), worked on his doctoral thesis at the Max Planck Institute for Biophysical Chemistry in the lab of British biochemist Victor P. Whittaker and received his PhD in medical science from the University of Göttingen in 1982. [Thomas Arthur Steitz](/wiki/Thomas_A._Steitz "Thomas A. Steitz") was a Macy Fellow at the University of Göttingen during 1976–1977.

Today, a number of judges in national and international courts of the highest level are still affiliated with the [Faculty of Law](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen_University_Faculty_of_Law "Göttingen University Faculty of Law"). As of 2021, four out of sixteen in-office Justices of the [Federal Constitutional Court](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court "Federal Constitutional Court") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bundesverfassungsgericht*; abbreviated: **BVerfG**), Germany's [supreme](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court") [constitutional court](/wiki/Constitutional_court "Constitutional court"), are affiliated with the University of Göttingen: two of them ([Andreas Paulus](/wiki/Andreas_Paulus "Andreas Paulus") & Christine Langenfeld) are, currently, professors at the Faculty of Law of the University of Göttingen, while two others (Ines Härtel & Henning Radtke) obtained their PhD in law (Dr.iur) from the University of Göttingen. Also in 2021, [Georg Nolte](/wiki/Georg_Nolte "Georg Nolte"), a former professor of public international law at the University of Göttingen, took office as [Judge of the International Court of Justice](/wiki/Judge_of_the_International_Court_of_Justice "Judge of the International Court of Justice") on behalf of the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany"). [Hsu Tzong-li](/wiki/Hsu_Tzong-li "Hsu Tzong-li"), a Taiwanese judge who has served as the President of the [Judicial Yuan](/wiki/Judicial_Yuan "Judicial Yuan") (Taiwan's constitutional court) since 2016, earned his doctorate in law from University of Göttingen in 1986.

* [Bernstein Network Computational Neuroscience](/wiki/Bernstein_Network_Computational_Neuroscience "Bernstein Network Computational Neuroscience")
* European Neuroscience Institute Göttingen (ENI-G)
* Fraunhofer Institute for Translational Medicine and Pharmacology (ITMP)
* Application Center for Plasma and Photonics of the Fraunhofer Institute for Surface Engineering and Thin Films IS
* [German Aerospace Center](/wiki/German_Aerospace_Center "German Aerospace Center")
* [German Center for Neurodegenerative Diseases](/wiki/German_Center_for_Neurodegenerative_Diseases "German Center for Neurodegenerative Diseases") (DZNE) – Helmholtz Centre
* [German Primate Center](/wiki/German_Primate_Center "German Primate Center") – Leibniz Institute for Primate Research
* [Max Planck Institute for Dynamics and Self-Organization](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Dynamics_and_Self-Organization "Max Planck Institute for Dynamics and Self-Organization"), formerly Max Planck Institute for Flow Research
* [Max Planck Institute for Multidisciplinary Sciences](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Multidisciplinary_Sciences "Max Planck Institute for Multidisciplinary Sciences"), a 2022 merger of formerly [Max Planck Institute for Biophysical Chemistry](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Biophysical_Chemistry "Max Planck Institute for Biophysical Chemistry") and formerly [Max Planck Institute for Experimental Medicine](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Experimental_Medicine "Max Planck Institute for Experimental Medicine")
* [Max Planck Institute for Solar System Research](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Solar_System_Research "Max Planck Institute for Solar System Research"), formerly [Max Planck Institute for Aeronomy](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Aeronomy "Max Planck Institute for Aeronomy")
* [Max Planck Institute for the Study of Religious and Ethnic Diversity](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_the_Study_of_Religious_and_Ethnic_Diversity "Max Planck Institute for the Study of Religious and Ethnic Diversity"), formerly [Max Planck Institute for History](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_History "Max Planck Institute for History")

The Alte Mensa

## Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Traditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Goettingen_Marktplatz_Oct06_Antilived.jpg/250px-Goettingen_Marktplatz_Oct06_Antilived.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goettingen_Marktplatz_Oct06_Antilived.jpg)

The Marktplatz in the centre of Goettingen town

The most famous tradition of the university is that PhD students who have just passed their *[Rigorosum](/wiki/Oral_exam "Oral exam")* (oral doctoral examination) or dissertation defense sit in a wagon – decorated with flowers and balloons and accompanied by relatives and friends, drive around the inner city and arrive at the Marktplatz – the central square where the old town hall and the [Gänseliesel](/wiki/G%C3%A4nseliesel "Gänseliesel") statue are located. The "newly born doctor" shall climb up to the statue of [Gänseliesel](/wiki/G%C3%A4nseliesel "Gänseliesel") (a poor princess in an old fairy tale who was compelled to keep geese by a wicked woman and later regained her identity), kiss the [Gänseliesel](/wiki/G%C3%A4nseliesel "Gänseliesel") and give bouquets to her.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Student life")]

There is an old saying about life in Göttingen, still inscribed in Latin nowadays on the wall of the entrance to the [Ratskeller](/wiki/Ratskeller "Ratskeller") (the restaurant located in the basement of the old town hall): [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Extra Gottingam non est vita, si est vita, non est ita* (*There is no life outside Göttingen. Even if it is life, it is no life like here*).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/5e/G%C3%B6ttingen_train_station_sign.jpg/250px-G%C3%B6ttingen_train_station_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:G%C3%B6ttingen_train_station_sign.jpg)

Sign at Göttingen train station displaying the motto *Stadt, die Wissen schafft* ("City that creates knowledge", playing also with the German word "[Wissenschaft](/wiki/Wissenschaft "Wissenschaft")", English "science")

> "Ancient university towns are wonderfully alike. Göttingen is like [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University") in England or [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") in America: very provincial, not on the way to anywhere – no one comes to these backwaters except for the company of professors. And the professors are sure that this is the centre of the world. There is an inscription in the Ratskeller there which reads 'Extra Gottingam non est vita', 'Outside Göttingen there is no life'. This epigram, or should I call it epitaph, is not taken as seriously by the undergraduates as by the professors."

— Bronowski, 1973, The Ascent of Man, p. 360

The university offers eight snack shops and six *Mensas* serving lunch at low prices for the students. One *Mensa* also provides dinner for students.

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notable people")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Göttingen people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen_people "List of University of Göttingen people").

Apart from those celebrities mentioned above, notable people that have studied or taught at Georg-August University include the American banker [J. P. Morgan](/wiki/J._P._Morgan "J. P. Morgan"), the seismologist [Beno Gutenberg](/wiki/Beno_Gutenberg "Beno Gutenberg"), the endocrinologist [Hakaru Hashimoto](/wiki/Hakaru_Hashimoto "Hakaru Hashimoto"), who studied there before [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and several notable [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_laureates "Nobel laureates") like [Max Planck](/wiki/Max_Planck "Max Planck") and [Werner Heisenberg](/wiki/Werner_Heisenberg "Werner Heisenberg"). Anthropologist [Marlina Flassy](/wiki/Marlina_Flassy "Marlina Flassy") earned her doctorate in Göttingen, before becoming the first woman and indigenous Papuan to be appointed Dean at [Cenderawasih University](/wiki/Cenderawasih_University "Cenderawasih University"). The German inventor of the jet engine, [Pabst von Ohain](/wiki/Pabst_von_Ohain "Pabst von Ohain"), also studied aerodynamics under [Ludwig Prandtl](/wiki/Ludwig_Prandtl "Ludwig Prandtl").
